title: Qardaha

infobox.value: Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun | Jul | Aug | Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec | Year

([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): القَرْدَاحَة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Qardāḥah*) is a town in northwestern [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), in the [mountains](/wiki/Syrian_Coastal_Mountain_Range "Syrian Coastal Mountain Range") overlooking the coastal town of [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia "Latakia"). Nearby localities include [Kilmakho](/wiki/Kilmakho "Kilmakho") to the west, [Bustan al-Basha](/wiki/Bustan_al-Basha "Bustan al-Basha") to the southwest, [Harf al-Musaytirah](/wiki/Harf_al-Musaytirah "Harf al-Musaytirah") to the southeast and [Muzayraa](/wiki/Muzayraa "Muzayraa") to the north. According to the [Syrian Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Central_Bureau_of_Statistics_(Syria) "Central Bureau of Statistics (Syria)"), Qardaha had a population of 8,671 in 2004.[[1]](#cite_note-CBS-1) It has a predominantly [Alawite](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites") population and is the traditional home of the [Assad family](/wiki/Assad_family "Assad family"),[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2) which ruled Syria from 1970 until 2024. It is believed that many of Assad’s loyalists have fled to Qardaha after the end of the Assad regime in December 2024.

[General Census of Population and Housing 2004](https://archive.today/20121221014214/http://www.cbssyr.org/new%20web%20site/General_census/census_2004/NH/TAB06-19-2004.htm). Town in Latakia, Syria

**Qardaha** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): القَرْدَاحَة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic "Romanization of Arabic"): *Qardāḥah*) is a town in northwestern [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), in the [mountains](/wiki/Syrian_Coastal_Mountain_Range "Syrian Coastal Mountain Range") overlooking the coastal town of [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia "Latakia"). Nearby localities include [Kilmakho](/wiki/Kilmakho "Kilmakho") to the west, [Bustan al-Basha](/wiki/Bustan_al-Basha "Bustan al-Basha") to the southwest, [Harf al-Musaytirah](/wiki/Harf_al-Musaytirah "Harf al-Musaytirah") to the southeast and [Muzayraa](/wiki/Muzayraa "Muzayraa") to the north. According to the [Syrian Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Central_Bureau_of_Statistics_(Syria) "Central Bureau of Statistics (Syria)"), Qardaha had a population of 8,671 in 2004.[[1]](#cite_note-CBS-1) It has a predominantly [Alawite](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites") population and is the traditional home of the [Assad family](/wiki/Assad_family "Assad family"),[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2) which ruled Syria from 1970 until 2024. It is believed that many of Assad’s loyalists have fled to Qardaha after the end of the Assad regime in December 2024.

[Syrian President](/wiki/President_of_Syria "President of Syria") [Hafez al-Assad](/wiki/Hafez_al-Assad "Hafez al-Assad"), who ruled from 1970 to 2000, was born in Qardaha. Under Assad, the government poured massive investments into Qardaha, Latakia and the surrounding region. Qardaha has many luxurious villas. A major statue of Hafez al-Assad used to exist in the town center, and a [huge mausoleum](/wiki/Assad_Mausoleum "Assad Mausoleum") containing the graves of [Bassel al-Assad](/wiki/Bassel_al-Assad "Bassel al-Assad") and Hafez al-Assad was also previously located there. [Hasan al-Khayer](/wiki/Hasan_al-Khayer "Hasan al-Khayer") was also born in Qardaha.

## Climate and geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Qardaha&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Climate and geography")]

Qardaha has a [hot-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*). Qardaha is in a mountainous area, but its altitude is only between 350 and 500 meters. It is in a beautiful forested area. Qardaha has much rainfall. The average high temperature in July is 29 °C, and in January is 7 °C. There is nearly 828 mm of rainfall annually and three days of snow in January. The climate and nature in Qardaha favour agriculture, and there are apple and orange plantations and tobacco farms.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Qardaha&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

According to written traditions, the residents of Qardaha descended from the [Kalbiyya](/wiki/Kalbiyya "Kalbiyya") tribal confederation and the town served as the confederation's principal center. During the late [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") era, between 1840 and 1880, tensions between the authorities and the Alawite tribes of the coastal mountains increased sharply. In 1854 the Ottoman governor of the Latakia Sanjak ("Latakia District") was killed in an armed confrontation between the authorities and members of the Qardaha-based tribe. This emboldened the Kalbiyya fighters, who proceeded to launch more raids against Ottoman positions, which the authorities responded to harshly.[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2)

Fear of attracting the attention and subjugation of the authorities, and avoiding taxation and military conscription, was one of the reasons many of the Alawite [fellahin](/wiki/Fellahin "Fellahin") ("peasants") who lived in the vicinity of Qardaha, opted not to establish an agglomeration of settlement. Until the present day, Qardaha is surrounded by scattered rural hamlets. The town formerly had a significant Christian population until the mid to late 20th-century, when most Christian families left for the major coastal cities.[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Mausoleum_of_Hafez_al-Assad_1.jpg/250px-Mausoleum_of_Hafez_al-Assad_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mausoleum_of_Hafez_al-Assad_1.jpg)

The interior of [Assad Mausoleum](/wiki/Assad_Mausoleum "Assad Mausoleum") before its destruction, with the main tomb in the center

In 1970, Qardaha was given city status, along with [al-Shaykh Badr](/wiki/Al-Shaykh_Badr "Al-Shaykh Badr") and [Duraykish](/wiki/Duraykish "Duraykish"). Together they made up around 6% of the total urban population of the two coastal governorates, Latakia and [Tartus](/wiki/Tartus_Governorate "Tartus Governorate"). The three towns were also designated the seats of newly formed [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Syria "Districts of Syria") (*[mantiqah](/wiki/Mantiqah "Mantiqah")*) centered around them.[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2) According to French anthropologist [Fabrice Balanche](/wiki/Fabrice_Balanche "Fabrice Balanche"), the [Ba'athist](/wiki/Ba%27ath_Party_(Syrian-dominated_faction) "Ba'ath Party (Syrian-dominated faction)") government which gained power in the 1960s, displayed a degree of favoritism for the three towns, all of which were Alawite, and Qardaha specifically. He particularly pointed to the fact that the subdistricts (*[nahiya](/wiki/Nahiya "Nahiya")*) of [al-Qadmus](/wiki/Al-Qadmus "Al-Qadmus") and [Mashta al-Helu](/wiki/Mashta_al-Helu "Mashta al-Helu"), predominantly Ismaili and Christian, respectively, were slated to become districts when each of their populations passed the 60,000 threshold, although the subdistricts of Qardaha, al-Shaykh Badr and Duraykish all had less than 40,000 inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-Balanche-2) The opening of civil and administrative services associated with its city status and state investments, such as the construction of a tobacco processing plant, spurred Qardaha's significant growth between 1970 and 1981, with an annual average of 4.9% compared to the coastal region's average of 3.8%.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBalanche2000248-4)

In early October 2012 during the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"), Mohammad al-Assad, cousin of President [Bashar al-Assad](/wiki/Bashar_al-Assad "Bashar al-Assad") and leader of the local Shabiha, was wounded after a gunfight in the town with a member of the rival Alawite Khayyir clan. This was sparked by a discussion about the earlier detention at [Damascus airport](/wiki/Damascus_airport "Damascus airport") of Abdel-Aziz Khayyer, a Qardaha native and member of the latter clan.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On 24 April 2013, the village was attacked with a barrage of rockets launched by rebels.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In early August 2013 in a surprise offensive, rebel fighters advanced south to the outskirts of the village of [Aramo](/wiki/Aramo "Aramo"), 20 km. (12 miles) from Qardaha. One of the attackers stated "The objective is to reach Qardaha and hurt them like they are hurting us."[[7]](#cite_note-7)

On 11 December 2024, the tombs of Hafez al-Assad and Bassel al-Assad were set on fire by rebel fighters.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
